description:
You are the on-call database reliability engineer.  
    Every night two CSV files are written under “/home/user/backups” — one produced by the primary
    PostgreSQL server, the other by its hot-stand-by replica.  
    A week of these nightly files (covering 2023-06-18 → 2023-06-24) has already been placed in the
    system for you.

    Your mission:

    1. Create the directory “/home/user/reports” (if it is not already present).
    2. From the seven *pairs* of nightly CSV files, build a single **weekly** report named  
       /home/user/reports/weekly_backup_status_20230618-20230624.csv
    3. The report **MUST** have exactly eight lines: one header line plus one data line for
       each of the seven dates.  
       a) Header line (must be the very first line, spelled exactly, comma-separated,
          no extra spaces):  
          Date,Tables_Backup,Size_MB,Duration,Copy_Start,Copy_End,Replica_Status  
       b) For every date, pull columns in this precise order:  
          • from the corresponding <date> file that starts with “primary_” take
            column 1 (Date), column 3 (Tables_Backup), column 4 (Size_MB), and column 5 (Duration);  
          • from the corresponding “replica_” file take column 3 (Copy_Start),
            column 4 (Copy_End) and column 5 (Status).  
       The seven data lines must appear in *ascending* date order
       (2023-06-18 first, 2023-06-24 last).
    4. Every line in the final CSV **must** end with a single UNIX newline (`\n`), including the
       very last line.  Do not add trailing spaces.
    5. While building the report, also create a tiny generation log at  
       /home/user/reports/weekly_backup_status_generation.log  
       containing exactly two lines (each ending with `\n`):  
       Lines_processed: 7  
       Report_path: /home/user/reports/weekly_backup_status_20230618-20230624.csv
    6. Only standard command-line utilities that exist in a minimal Debian/Ubuntu installation
       may be used (e.g. `cut`, `paste`, `sort`, `cat`, `printf`, shell redirection, etc.).
       You do *not* need sudo rights.

    Verification checklist (what the autograder will look for):
    • the directory “/home/user/reports” exists;  
    • the file “weekly_backup_status_20230618-20230624.csv” exists, is readable,
      and contains exactly eight lines;  
    • the first line is the header shown above, byte-for-byte;  
    • data lines are in ascending chronological order and each has exactly six commas
      (i.e. seven fields);  
    • field values for every date match those in the source nightly CSVs;  
    • the generation log exists **and** its contents match the two-line format
      described in step 5.

    Hints you may find useful (do not rely on any single hint exclusively):
      • a combination of `cut -d ',' -f …` and `paste -d ',' …` is often the quickest way
        to merge columns horizontally;  
      • be mindful that header rows must be excluded when cutting real data;  
      • consider looping over the files in parallel so the primary and replica
        for a given date are processed side-by-side.

    Remember: only the two deliverables inside “/home/user/reports” are graded.  
    No additional output, logging, or files are expected.
truth:
